As evil leader on round 1, the optimal play is usually to propose a plausible team that can pass votes and avoid immediately exposing the evil pair. Sending myself with a good player is standard because a first-quest fail creates early pressure and suspicion can often be managed, while not pairing both evils avoids outing us if the team is later scrutinized. This reveals little beyond a normal-looking opener and keeps flexibility for future rounds.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: No concrete role reads yet; as a Servant on round 1 with team [0,1], early standard play is usually to approve inclusive first teams, especially when I am on it, to gather information and avoid unnecessary rejections. My vote reveals little beyond a conventional good preference for testing the first proposal, and rejecting here gives up an information opportunity.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 4 are good. The proposed team [0,1] includes me but not my teammate. On round 1 quest 1, as evil, getting myself onto the team is good because I can fail immediately and create confusion, while an approve vote is natural since I proposed this team. Approving reveals little because leaders usually support their own teams, and rejecting my own team would be suspicious and needlessly reduce evil influence on the quest.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 is Evil; 3 is also Evil, with Mordred hidden. The proposed team [0,1] includes a known Evil player, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending an Evil on quest 1. Voting reject signals some suspicion of 1, which may slightly expose me later, but preventing an early bad team is worth it.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,1] contains one evil and one unknown. On team voting as evil, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing at least one evil so we have a chance to fail the quest and gain control early. Approving also looks natural on round 1 and does not reveal much, while rejecting a team with my teammate could deny evil an early opportunity.
Player 4: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting history. As a Good Servant on round 1, a common optimal approach is to approve an initial 2-person team unless there is specific suspicion, since early approvals generate information and avoid needless rejection pressure. My vote reveals little beyond a standard good-faith willingness to test an opening team, which helps Good more than an arbitrary reject here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I only know quest 1 team [1,0] succeeded, which makes 1 and 0 somewhat more trustworthy but does not clear anyone. Team [0,2,4] includes me plus two unknowns. As Good, on quest 2 with little evidence, approving a team that includes a proven-success member (me) is reasonable; rejecting gives little extra info and risks stalling. My vote reveals modest trust in a mixed but acceptable team, which is fine for Good at this stage.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,2,4] contains no known evil and would likely produce another success, strengthening the good side. My own previous team [1,0] succeeded with 0 fails because I chose not to fail early, which keeps suspicion low on me. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is usually to reject clean all-good teams, especially when neither evil is included, to prevent good from stacking easy successes. My vote itself reveals limited information since evil can still sometimes approve to blend in, but rejecting here is strategically better to deny a likely good success.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil, so 0,2,4 contains no known evil from my perspective and is a strong good team. In team voting, I want to approve teams likely to succeed while not making my knowledge too obvious. Approving my own team of 0,2,4 is optimal and consistent with a good player trying to build on the prior success without exposing Merlin information.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,2,4] excludes both evil players, so it is very likely an all-good team that would succeed and give Good a 2-0 lead. As evil in team voting, the optimal play is to reject strong good-looking teams, especially ones with no evil on them. My reject vote is aligned with preventing a clean good quest and does not reveal much, since rejecting a 3-person team after a success is not unusual.
Player 4: Quest 1 team [1,0] succeeded, so 0 and 1 each look somewhat good, though one could still be evil. Proposed team [0,2,4] includes me and 0 from a successful team, and only swaps in 2. As a good Servant in early game, approving a team with one previously cleared-ish member and myself is reasonable; rejecting gives little extra info and risks stalling. My approve vote is mildly informative but not overly so.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,2,4] mean player 0 (me) is strongly good, and there is some positive evidence on 1,2,4 since no fails appeared. Team [0,1] already succeeded once, so approving is low risk. As a good Servant in team voting, optimal play is to support teams with proven success members, especially on quest 3 where only one fail is needed in a 5-player game. Approving reveals consistency with prior evidence-based play and helps good lock in a strong team.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1] contains me but not my teammate. Player 0 has been on both successful quests, which often makes them look good; that can help evil if they continue to be trusted. In team voting, as evil and down 0-2, I want to maximize chances to get an evil player onto missions without making my alignment obvious. Approving a 2-player team with only me as evil gives us a chance to fail this quest and keep the game alive; rejecting risks a different team that may exclude both evils. My approval is not too revealing because I was also on the successful first quest and can claim consistency.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes a known evil, so from Good's perspective this is a bad team even though quest 1 happened to succeed with 1 on it, likely due to an intentional pass. In team voting, I should reject teams containing known evil while avoiding giving away too much; a reject here is standard and helps keep quest 3 on an all-good team if possible. My vote reveals little because rejecting a team with a suspicious member is natural.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Team [0,1] includes exactly one known evil and one player, 0, who has been on both successful quests, so 0 is broadly trusted. As Minion on quest 3 with evil down 0-2, the optimal play is to get evil onto teams and preserve cover; approving a team with my teammate on it is good. Rejecting my own proposed team would look suspicious and slow us without clear benefit. My vote reveals some alignment info, but approving is the natural and least harmful signal here.
Player 4: Two successful quests with zero fails strongly suggest players 0 and 1 are likely good, and player 0 has now been on both successful teams. As a Servant, optimal play is to approve a 2-player team of likely good players, especially early in round 1 with a strong success record. Approving supports a high-probability success team and reveals little harmful information beyond consistency with trusting proven successful members.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest history strongly implicates Player 1 or 3 from the failed [0,1] team, since [0,2,4] succeeded twice with no fails, making 2 and 4 likely good and myself good. For quest 4 needing 3 players, [0,2,4] is the best-known good team and should be approved. Approving supports a highly likely all-good team and reveals little harmful information.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 3 team [0,1] failed with exactly 1 fail, and since I was on it, that fail could have been me; this gives some suspicion on me and/or 0. Team [0,2,4] has already succeeded once with 0 fails, so it is likely viewed as a strong good team. As evil on team voting for quest 4 at 2-1, the optimal play is to reject a likely all-good team that could win the game for Good if approved and passed. My vote itself reveals limited information; rejecting is consistent with evil trying to block a strong-looking team, and even if noticed, stopping this team is more important.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. Proposed team [0,2,4] excludes both known evil players and is exactly the team that produced two earlier successes, so it is the strongest good team. In team voting, approving this maximizes good's chance to win quest 4. My vote reveals little because this is the obviously strongest team from my perspective and also from public results.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Team [0,2,4] is the exact same trio that produced a clean success on Quest 2, so it likely contains no evil players and excludes both evils. At 2-1 with Quest 4 being pivotal, as evil I want to prevent a likely good-only team from going. Rejecting also keeps suspicion aligned with prior uncertainty, since a known-success trio can still be reasonably opposed by evil without exposing extra role info. My vote itself reveals little because team votes are not individually public in the log shown; even if they are, rejecting a team that excludes me and my teammate is standard evil play and helps my side more than approving a probable success.
Player 4: Players 0,2,4 were on two successful quests together, while quest 3 with team 0,1 failed with exactly one fail, making player 1 highly suspicious and 0 somewhat less likely but still possible only if 1 is evil. As a Good Servant, the optimal play is to approve the proven-success team [0,2,4], especially on quest 4 where securing a success would win for Good. Approving reveals consistency with backing the strongest available team and does not meaningfully help Evil more than rejecting would.
